skill.name: Azure Cosmos DB SDK for Rust
skill.description: Azure Cosmos DB SDK for Rust documentation and usage patterns for NoSQL API operations
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for using Azure Cosmos DB Rust SDK with document CRUD, queries, and global distribution
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains only documentation for Azure Cosmos DB Rust SDK usage. Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 security findings and risk score 0/100. The SKILL.md file provides legitimate instructional content for Microsoft's official azure_data_cosmos crate with no executable code or security concerns.

content.user_title: Build Azure Cosmos DB Applications with Rust
content.value_statement: Developers need to work with globally distributed databases efficiently. This skill provides comprehensive Azure Cosmos DB Rust SDK guidance for document operations, queries, and container management.
actual_capabilities: Guide document CRUD operations (create, read, update, delete) with Azure Cosmos DB
Explain partition key usage and container operations
Demonstrate Entra ID authentication with DeveloperToolsCredential
Show patch operations for partial document updates
Provide query patterns for NoSQL API
Explain client hierarchy and reuse patterns
limitations: Documentation only - requires Rust project setup and Azure subscription
Covers NoSQL API only - does not include MongoDB, Cassandra, or Gremlin APIs
Requires azure_data_cosmos and azure_identity crates installation
Authentication examples assume Azure CLI or developer tools configuration

use_cases.title: Backend Developer Building Cloud-Native Apps
use_cases.description: Learn to integrate Azure Cosmos DB into Rust microservices with proper authentication, partitioning, and efficient query patterns for globally distributed data.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers working with Azure cloud services

use_cases.title: Database Engineer Migrating to Rust
use_cases.description: Understand Cosmos DB Rust SDK patterns including item serialization, deserialization, and optimal container configuration for production workloads.
use_cases.target_user: Database professionals adopting Rust for data access layers

use_cases.title: Solutions Architect Designing Multi-Region Systems
use_cases.description: Explore Cosmos DB global distribution capabilities with Rust SDK for building low-latency, highly available applications across geographic regions.
use_cases.target_user: Technical architects planning distributed system architectures

prompt_templates.title: Basic Item Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create, read, update, and delete items in Azure Cosmos DB using Rust SDK with proper error handling and partition key configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Authentication Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to authenticate to Azure Cosmos DB using Entra ID credentials in Rust. Include DeveloperToolsCredential setup and configuration for local development.

prompt_templates.title: Query Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Provide examples of efficient query patterns for Azure Cosmos DB Rust SDK including FILTER, ORDER BY, and cross-partition queries with best practices.

prompt_templates.title: Production Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete Rust module for Cosmos DB operations including connection pooling, retry policies, custom error types, and structured logging for production use.

output_examples.input: Create a document with id, partition key, and custom fields
output_examples.output: Define a struct with Serialize/Deserialize derives, instantiate with required fields, and call container.create_item() with partition key and item. Use into_model() to deserialize responses.

output_examples.input: Update specific fields without replacing entire document
output_examples.output: Use PatchDocument to specify add and remove operations on JSON paths. Call container.patch_item() with partition key, item id, and patch document configuration.

best_practices: Always specify partition key for point reads and writes to optimize RU consumption
Reuse CosmosClient and DatabaseClient instances - they are thread-safe and designed for sharing
Prefer Entra ID authentication over key-based auth for production environments
Derive Serialize and Deserialize traits for all document structs
Use into_model() to properly deserialize Cosmos DB responses into your types
anti_patterns: Creating new client instances for each operation - causes unnecessary connection overhead
Omitting partition key in queries - results in cross-partition scans and higher costs
Using key-based authentication in production - less secure than managed identity or service principals
Storing large documents without considering 2MB item size limit

faq.question: How do I authenticate to Azure Cosmos DB locally?
faq.answer: Use DeveloperToolsCredential from azure_identity crate. Install Azure CLI first and run 'az login'. The credential automatically uses your Azure CLI identity for authentication.

faq.question: What is the difference between replace_item and patch_item?
faq.answer: replace_item sends the complete document to Cosmos DB. patch_item uses RFC 6902 JSON Patch operations to modify specific fields, which is more efficient for partial updates.

faq.question: Do I need to handle retry logic for Cosmos DB operations?
faq.answer: The azure_data_cosmos SDK includes built-in retry logic for transient failures. You can configure retry policies through client options if custom behavior is needed.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with Cosmos DB MongoDB API?
faq.answer: No, this skill covers the NoSQL API only. MongoDB API requires different drivers and query patterns. Use MongoDB-compatible Rust drivers for MongoDB API accounts.

faq.question: How do I handle Cosmos DB errors in Rust?
faq.answer: SDK methods return Result types. Match on error variants to handle specific cases like NotFound, Conflict, or RateLimited. Use the error() method on responses for details.

faq.question: Is the Cosmos DB Rust SDK suitable for production?
faq.answer: Yes, azure_data_cosmos is a production-ready Microsoft-supported SDK. Ensure proper configuration of retry policies, timeouts, and connection pooling for production workloads.
